note: EUR-Lex CELEX 32019R1781 URL is a valid official source for Regulation (EU) 2019/1781. IEC 60034-30-1:2014 defining IE1–IE4 classes confirmed published and widely cited. IEC TS 60034-30-2:2016 confirmed as a published IEC Technical Specification covering variable-speed motor types. GB 18613-2020 confirmed mandatory from 2021-06-01 via SAMR. Gap note (GB 18613 IE3 ≠ IEC 60034-30-1 IE3 limit values at all power/pole points) is well-documented in technical literature. IE2 permitted only with VSD claim was accurate under 640/2009 but Regulation 2019/1781 eliminates the IE2+VSD exception — this row does not assert that exception so no error, but the summary sentence 'IE2 permitted only when used with variable speed drives' reflects the old 640/2009 exception that is now abolished under 2019/1781; flag as uncertain pending editorial review.

SOURCE URL IS WRONG: https://webstore.iec.ch/en/publication/141 resolves to IEC 60034-5:2000/COR1:2001 (a corrigendum for IP protection degrees), NOT IEC 60034-2-1:2014. The correct IEC webstore URL for IEC 60034-2-1:2014 is https://webstore.iec.ch/en/publication/121. URL has been corrected in this record. Standard itself (IEC 60034-2-1:2014) confirmed to exist and be the mandatory test method referenced in Annex II of Regulation (EU) 2019/1781. GB/T 1032-2012 confirmed as the Chinese equivalent test standard. Stray-load-loss gap is technically accurate per industry sources.

CRITICAL GAP ERROR: The row states China's legal MEPS floor is Grade 3 (~IE2) and the gap is EU=IE3 vs CN=IE2. This is factually wrong. GB 18613-2020 Grade 3 corresponds to IE3 (NOT IE2). Multiple authoritative sources confirm: 'IE3 efficiency became the lowest energy efficiency limit (Level 3) for three-phase asynchronous motors in China from 2021-06-01; motors below IE3 are not allowed to be produced or sold.' Grade mapping is: Grade 1 ≈ IE4/IE5, Grade 2 ≈ IE4, Grade 3 = IE3 (the minimum). The described gap ('EU floor = IE3, CN floor = IE2') is incorrect; both floors are IE3 from the same approximate date. The actual remaining gap is: (a) efficiency limit values in GB 18613 and IEC 60034-30-1 are not identical for all power/pole combinations; (b) test method differences (GB/T 1032 vs IEC 60034-2-1) may mean a GB-tested IE3 motor does not meet IEC-tested IE3 limits. Additionally, the row's cn_common_equivalent text correctly identifies Grade 2 as '~IE3' and Grade 3 as '~IE2', which contradicts the mandatory standard itself — this internal inconsistency is an error. Source URL confirmed valid.

IE4 mandatory for 75–200 kW (2-/4-/6-pole, 50 Hz) from 1 July 2023 confirmed by multiple official and industry sources including IEA, IEA-4E, Nidec/Control Techniques, and ABB. Regulation (EU) 2019/1781 Annex I paragraph 1(b) is the correct citation point. [UNVERIFIED] tags in the source data are appropriately cautious. GB 18613-2020 Grade 1 ≈ IE4/IE5 as voluntary tier confirmed. The statement 'Grade 1 ≈ IE4' is slightly imprecise (some sources map Grade 1 to IE5 in the 2020 standard), flagged for human review. Source URL confirmed valid official EUR-Lex.

IEC 60034-2-1 as the sole accepted EU test method for Ecodesign efficiency determination confirmed via Regulation (EU) 2019/1781 Article 3 and Annex I. GB/T 1032-2012 confirmed as the Chinese equivalent, substantially harmonised but not fully identical (stray-load loss treatment difference is well-documented). Gap description is accurate. Source URL confirmed valid. Note: IEC 60034-2-1 has been updated to a 2024 edition; the 2014 version referenced remains the baseline for current EU compliance documentation, but human review of whether the 2024 update affects EU conformity obligations is advisable.

EMC Directive 2014/30/EU confirmed as existing and the source URL (CELEX 32014L0030) is a valid EUR-Lex identifier. EN IEC 61800-3:2018 confirmed as a published harmonised standard under EMC Directive 2014/30/EU covering adjustable-speed power drive systems. The 2018 edition has since been superseded by EN IEC 61800-3:2023, but this does not invalidate the row's content for current compliance purposes as transition periods apply. The claim that a bare passive induction motor is generally outside EMC Directive scope is consistent with EU guidance. GB/T 12668.3-2012 confirmed as equivalent to IEC 61800-3:2004 (one revision behind 2018). Gap analysis is accurate.

EN IEC 61800-3:2018 confirmed as harmonised standard under EMC Directive 2014/30/EU for motor+VFD systems. C1/C2/C3/C4 environment categories confirmed. GB/T 12668.3-2012 confirmed as equivalent to IEC 61800-3:2004 (not the current 2018/2023 edition). Gap analysis (mandatory EU CE vs voluntary CN GB/T) is accurate. Source URL (CELEX 32014L0030) confirmed valid EUR-Lex identifier. The note about the 2023 supersession of the 2018 edition means 'EN IEC 61800-3:2018' in the row may need updating to :2023 for current harmonised standard reference; flagging for human review.

LVD 2014/35/EU source URL (CELEX 32014L0035) confirmed as valid official EUR-Lex identifier. EN 60034-1:2010+A1:2017, EN 60034-5, and EN 60034-6 confirmed as published and used as harmonised standards under LVD. GB 755-2019 confirmed as equivalent to IEC 60034-1. Gap analysis (procedural CE marking obligations vs no Chinese equivalent) is accurate. The statement 'CCC certification is not currently mandatory for general industrial motors' is consistent with known CNCA catalogue exemptions, though the [UNVERIFIED] caveat in moteu-001 is appropriate. Note: EN 60034-1 has been updated; EN IEC 60034-1:2026 exists — human review of whether the specific amendment references cited remain the active harmonised versions is advisable.

STATUS/CONTENT ERROR: The row states 'IE2 for motors 75–200 kW when operated with a variable speed drive' as a current EU requirement. This is incorrect under Regulation (EU) 2019/1781 as implemented from July 2023: the IE2+VFD exception from the predecessor Regulation 640/2009 was abolished by 2019/1781. From 1 July 2023, motors 75–200 kW must meet IE4 (not IE2 with VFD). The VSD itself must meet IE2 efficiency per Annex I paragraph 2 of 2019/1781, but the motor is not permitted to drop to IE2 simply because it is paired with a VSD. This error could mislead exporters into underestimating the EU requirement for this power range. Source URL confirmed valid. IEC 60034-30-1 and EN 60034-5 standards confirmed. Remainder of gap analysis is accurate.

LVD 2014/35/EU and EMC Directive 2014/30/EU source URLs confirmed as valid EUR-Lex identifiers. EN 60034-1 as harmonised standard under LVD confirmed. Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 on market surveillance confirmed. The [UNVERIFIED] note about CCC catalogue exclusion is appropriately cautious — CCC exemption for general industrial 3-phase motors is widely documented but the exact scope boundary should be verified against current CNCA catalogue. Gap and mandatory_status content is accurate. GB/T 10069 series for EMC of rotating machines is referenced but not directly verifiable without SAMR/SAC access; flagged as uncertain.

Regulation (EU) 2019/1781 source URL confirmed. Scope expansion to 0.12–0.75 kW (IE2) from July 2023 confirmed. IE3 for 0.75–1000 kW from July 2021 confirmed. IEC 60034-2-1 and IEC 60034-30-1 standards confirmed. GB 18613-2020 effective date 2021-06-01 confirmed. The cn_common_equivalent correctly states Grade 2 ≈ IE3 and Grade 3 as minimum (IE3), which is consistent with corrected understanding of GB 18613-2020. The [UNVERIFIED] note about exact scope vs power boundary differences is appropriately cautious. Gap analysis regarding nameplate/website multi-load-point efficiency publication is a known EU Ecodesign requirement not present in GB 18613. Overall content is accurate.

Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 source URL (CELEX 32019R1020) confirmed as valid EUR-Lex identifier. The July 16, 2021 enforcement date for EU-based responsible person requirement confirmed. Obligation for EU Authorised Representative for non-EU manufacturers confirmed by multiple compliance service sources. LVD Art.10 and 2019/1781 Art.8 references are plausible standard article locations — not independently clause-verified but consistent with the regulation structure. Gap analysis (no Chinese equivalent to EU AR concept) is accurate.
